item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid I'll have to downvote. While I absolutely love the meta angle of it, considered in universe I just can't justify its existence. No matter how I slice it, this SCP could at best exist for a brief flash of time. Pretty sure there are more than enough things the foundation could employ to destroy it, let alone detect the writer was working on it before it was ever uploaded.

In addition, while there's no problem with the researcher being a jackass in and of itself, the last line pushes it over the edge for me, because you're trying to sell me that a doctor who is far enough gone to say something like that didn't raise any red flags to foundation personal? Its bad enough that the foundation apparently didn't consider it important to observe the doctor who suggested combining a meme with an infohazard after her funding was denied, but you mean to tell me that someone who was far enough gone to do something like this didn't say or do anything beforehand worth having an eye kept on her?

Idk, I know a lot of people like or don't mind the foundation being portrayed as incompetent, but I've never cared for it, especially if it involves memes or info-hazards, since it immediately raises questions of how on earth the foundation wasn't destroyed 10 times over by now. If perhaps a bit more was added to explain how the doctor wasn't caught beforehand or the SCP destroyed, I could get behind it, but as it is it's all a bit much for my suspension of disbelief.

(Unlike some other critics of this piece, I won't disparage your writing skills in and of themselves or imply this should have been a -J, because it shouldn't be. I may feel this piece needs some tweaks, but overall there's a good concept here that's definitely worth doing, and it was well written enough that despite my numerous issues with it I almost no voted instead of down voting.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was torn between novote and upvote. Ultimately upvoted. Isn't my cup of tea, but is very well done. It does need a full read to actually be taken seriously, though, and that almost lost me.

item.title: First Upvote
item.content:encoded: I tend to keep away from commenting on things, liking/disliking/voting on things, etc, in most medians. I make an exception here due to my personal love for well written meta. A lot of people seem to not understand metaskipping, or downvote a page sheerly for it being a metaskip, even though metaskips, when done right, can provide the best narrative possible, as they aren't bound by much of anything. You, you did it well. As a result, have my first upvote.

item.title: Re: Uuuuh something seems to be wrong with the document.
item.content:encoded: You're missing the entire point. The SCP is its own SCP document. And, it was created by a disgruntled memetics specialist specifically to fuck with the Foundation. The random characters are part of the memetic effects. If you actually read it, it'd all be spelled out for you. This isn't so highly rated because we're all imbeciles and upvote everything. It's highly rated because it's an entertaining piece of fiction.

item.title: Re: Uuuuh something seems to be wrong with the document.
item.content:encoded:
Last edit was 199 days ago at the time of this post to add in bavigation links, and no suspicious activity in the history.

What you're reading can be called a format screw, wherein the SCP purposefully violates reader expectations regarding the format with in-universe reasons that are well-executed.

item.title: Re: Uuuuh something seems to be wrong with the document.
item.content:encoded:
There are no unauthorized edits to this page, that I can see.

If you feel it doesn't belong, downvote it.

Nothing's been vandalized.

item.title: Uuuuh something seems to be wrong with the document.
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if this was the creator's doing, but the document seems to be vandalized.
Many letters are replaced with numbers, and the whole document looks like it just shouldn't be on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) The article doesn't need any changes.

2) The usage of 2, 3, 5, and 7 is a reference to the Item # (2357) and is justified in-universe as memetic triggers necessary to produce the desired effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) This really feels like it should be a -J. It's a brilliant piece, but it undermines the professionality of the Foundation and the fact that I was not experiencing the effects described by the piece completely broke my immersion.

2) Are the numb3rs a 5pecific r3ference, 0r just thr0wn in th3re for sh1t5 and g1gg1es? Because if it's the former I would really appreciate an explanation :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I got to thinking about how low the odds are of this meme getting out, and was struck with a small idea. The Foundation is multi-national and exempt from much of the scrutiny of world Governments while at the same time, any and all "shadow agencies" have rumors about their tactics; of which they may or may not be true.

As such, perhaps a situation could happen where the requirements to be able to spread the meme are met "just enough" for it to break its intended containment. How would that phone call sound? So I came up with: http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/akiva this audio transcription I posted to my sandbox. If you like it Commie, may it be added as an addendum to your already Perfect SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't even thought of doing that, and it's pretty cool that the result is interesting. There are certainly worse story hooks out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if anyone else thought to look, but I turned the visible 1337-speak into a Lat/Long coordinate (37.2557 N 22.7532357 E) and came up with a location in Leonidio, Greece. It's just north of a small copse of trees arranged in a circle with an open area in the center. Possible story hook there as well!

Edited to add: The location is in the mountains far from any civilization, great place for a hidden base? Or perhaps it's the site the doctor resigned from? I dunno, do as you will!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, it's perfect the way it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should have been in the joke section :/.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I was wondering the future writing because of the history contest tale that uses Dr. Graff…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some SCPs set in the future. There is no rule saying that SCPs have to be set in the past or the present.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: iunno

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this set in the future?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, author here. I don't think your post holds any weight at all, and I'm not going to change this SCP or how I write any future SCPs on account of it.

Not all authors are going to recognize superficial, poorly thought-out critique for what it is, so I strongly recommend putting more effort into reading and evaluating SCPs in the future.

"4th wall breaks" are countable. Go for number, not amount.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is practically a shitpost. The amount of 4th wall breaks makes it a better creepypasta-wiki page than an actual SCP. I agree with Aelanna, this is borderline LOLFoundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Insert necessary tidus laugh here.

This is great. 1+ , Anomoly or otherwise, Just seeing this for a few seconds made me almost do a tidus laugh in the middle of my class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ironically it not consider as a -J SCP or a Guidelines to make SCP articles.

item.title: Re: Hmmmm...
item.content:encoded:
Well, SCPs don't have to be always scary. Yes, SCPs do have origins in the horror genre. But we have over 2000 articles, and it would be a bore if every last SCP is yet another horror story. One may get de-sensitised over time.

While this may not be horror, it adds favour to the SCP Foundation world and makes it distinct from its creepypasta roots and make it its own franchise.

item.title: Hmmmm...
item.content:encoded: This is a good article…but I don't find it scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apparantly, I downvoted this. And forgot about it.

So I reread it.

Still downvoted, because this is basically a temper-tantrum by someone who got a little bit to big for her britches and decided to screw with the Foundation as a final "Fuck you". I understand the sentimentality, but the whole thing leaves a bad taste in my mouth. As stated upthread, this is a character-driven SCP, and I can't upvote this as I despise the character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally like to think that Dr. Graff COMPLETELY FAILED in her plan and just didn't realize it. While the document does use memes to encourage readers to finish (and at least some readers will desire to share it with others), there is nothing compulsory about it. If anything, SCP-2357 is more effective out of universe than in: we're all here looking for neat SCPs, while a Foundation researcher would see and report this for deletion at once. So all she did was prove that her research wasn't going to work anyways and saved the Foundation the trouble of having to give her a severance package. Probably not your intent, but that's my current headcanon.
Anyways, I'd upvote, but that would be doing what Graff wants, so standard anti-meme procedures dictate that I do not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's basically "memetic framework please ignore" — necessary for it to work, but should not actually be observed. Or maybe it's semi-relevant gibberish, who knows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what it means, I think it's a part of the SCP itself.
As you probably already expected, this SCP is an exception of almost all other SCP's.
I know only one other SCP with a similar tone and that is SCP-426.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Please ignore the memetic bounds/borders"? What's that supposed to mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice, I found the a hidden message in this SCP:
If you look at the revisions of this SCP and view the scource of this SCP, you will find at the rating module something like this; (If someone else didn't explore this before I did)

+ show block
– hide block

(ignore the spacing…)

[ [ > ] ]
[ [ s i z e 1 % ] ] # # w h i t e | M 3 M E T I S C ^ ^ 2 ^ ^ E N R A H M E N 7 G N O R I E R E N 5 I E B I T T E # # [ [ / s i z e ] ]
[ [ m o d u l e R a t e ] ]
[ [ / > ] ]


Of course this deserves an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's anomalous. That's the base requirement for being an SCP.

Try reading through the other comments in this discussion thread if you're confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It… Exactly how is this an SCP again? It's a memetic. That's all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea and excellent execution

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm just not sure how "she might fail" is a rebuttal to anything presented in the article. It may indicate that she might be overconfident, yes, but she doesn't believe that, and she's the one with the mic. The suggestion that you write a tale about it was sincere.

As I'm pretty sure I've stated upthread, this is a character piece, and I'm not overly concerned about whether or not there are holes in the character's plans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She doesn't have the security clearance to even know that most of them exist, for starters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't tell if you're being serious or passive-aggressive or just mimicking my intended droll tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Operating under the theory that part of the meme here is rooted in subtlety

Nope.

Seriously though, I'm pretty sure the Foundation is going to employ memeticists who are NOT familiar with SCP formatting in some circumstances, at which point this whole thing falls apart as they point out that everyone is being affected by a memetic hazard and they clean it the heck up.

Maybe they do. Who knows. You should write a tale about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those will be contacted the last just so the rest of the Foundation gets the document. Our rebellious memeticist will have thought of everything. Yes I want her to win so what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whatever, the secondary condition was familiarity with standard SCP documentation formatting. I'm sure we have a memeticist somewhere on hand who is not familiar with SCP documentation formatting (not to mention several inhuman somethings) to point it out and get rid of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Operating under the theory that part of the meme here is rooted in subtlety, I will now break your meme by posting relevant fragments.

Without the rest of the text, these are nothing. The moment you try to discover how they interact with the rest of the document, either you snap the text in different sentences and give them to different memeticists so they can analyse them separately (which defeats the purpose of the analysis, since without all the portions the memetic effect is lost or degraded to a point no analysis might be possible), or the memeticists themselves get infected.

To be honest, memeticists should work with computers to check and re-check what memetic effects exist in a document, identifying them prior to actual reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Operating under the theory that part of the meme here is rooted in subtlety, I will now break your meme by posting relevant fragments.
Near-invisible:
M3METISC2EN RAHMEN 7GNORIEREN 5IE BITTE
Main document:
e to 3
l to 7
to to 2
s to 5
(no character) to 5
t to 7
FOOTNOTE GOES HERE
to to 2
(no character) to 2
ss to ssss7ssss5sssss3sss
(no character) to 2
e to 3
s to 5
t to 7

Footnote contents:
e to 3
ff to ffffg^g^g^g^

Personally, I caught all the little things immediately as I read the article. I kept reading not because I was compelled to, but because I chose to finish this childish excuse for a resignation letter. I will not be sharing this with anyone, nor do I find it particularly well-written. I mean, the format screw is okay, but it's just not quite clicking. I'll still upvote it because I upvote anything that's even halfway decent, but perhaps the miss should consider that trying something inhuman is far from impossible for the Foundation, and that this document would have been secured as soon as any inhuman foundation employee (of whom there are at least a few) witnessed it, or even anyone who knew shit about memetics but nothing about SCP classification formatting. That said, this need only be a snapshot of the document at some point in time, but still, I'm pretty sure it will be properly expunged from record and properly contained within the year. Yes, this comment seems to be preparing to jump back into the bounds of the fourth wall.

Seriously though, I'm pretty sure the Foundation is going to employ memeticists who are NOT familiar with SCP formatting in some circumstances, at which point this whole thing falls apart as they point out that everyone is being affected by a memetic hazard and they clean it the heck up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The worst part is that this is actually one of the effects of the object.

item.title: Re: So...was this created by a Black Hat or a Grey Hat?
item.content:encoded: They are too busy telling you about how awesome this document they just got is. You should frame it and place it in a perfectly visible and well-lit locale, too.

item.title: So...was this created by a Black Hat or a Grey Hat?
item.content:encoded: What does the Ethics Committee have to say about potentially irresponsible memetic-hacking?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah. Restrictions can breed creativity. I'm pro-restrictions, for the most part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this skip a lot. For some reason though I get the sneaking suspicion that this is a subtle dig at a few of our esteemed authors views on being constrained to a strict writing format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While everyone else keeps questioning the plausibility of such a smart, flexible and high-level profile Foundation researcher being too petulant and immature, I will say that she kind of has a point.

Although she still is petulant and immature, of course. But the fact that she actually managed to put a self-recursive memetic effect inside a self-referencing article sounds like a master piece of anomalous art and does point out a major flaw in memetic protection procedures for all personnel.

So, yeah, it's cool. Upvoted.
Aw damn when did that happen? I missed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an original character (do not steal).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking genius. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Then the question becomes, why did the Foundation hire someone so unhinged and childish? Well, one, clearly she's brilliant and great at making skips. Two, maybe she wasn't ALWAYS unhinged, and three, its possible that the Foundation with its hugeness sometimes misses some key, subtle personnel problems. Doesn't mean the whole thing is bad at its job.

It's a combination of all three. She -is- brilliant — if she wasn't, they wouldn't let her near the memes. Letting someone with an unusual mind work with memes long enough isn't strictly healthy for their psyche, but the Foundation wouldn't get anything done if they had to hire a new memeticist every time one of the old ones had an unusual psych eval. And they made the mistake of not realizing that they had let her particular occupational hazard-induced emotional hangups progress too far, and thus they didn't bother to run damage control or appease her when they denied the most important proposal she made in her career.

And, well, from there, she got pissed, hacked together SCP-2357, said "sayonara", and left for greener pastures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

So is this supposed to be one of the characters from the Cool War? Because I'm not seeing who she is. Carol, maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can finish writing that post up later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only piece you're missing here is the one you glossed right over: she _is_ childish. But she was brilliant so the Foundation overlooked her childishness for too long.

I don't think the author is speaking Meta here, I think he is trying to write some brat who (stupidly) feels like she was undermined and ignored, when in reality, she probably just needed to talk to the right people or make her report a little more persuasive.

Then the question becomes, why did the Foundation hire someone so unhinged and childish? Well, one, clearly she's brilliant and great at making skips. Two, maybe she wasn't ALWAYS unhinged, and three, its possible that the Foundation with its hugeness sometimes misses some key, subtle personnel problems. Doesn't mean the whole thing is bad at its job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's probably memetic or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm obviously missing something. What was the point of the 1337 speak?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clever idea. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instinct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesomeness! In that case I would like to add you as a contact so that I can show you a revised version to confirm once I've finished.
